responses: We built an AI system that manages dynamic pricing for a last-mile delivery marketplace connecting 12,000 independent couriers with 800 merchant partners across 6 metropolitan markets. The system determines courier compensation rates, delivery fee pricing to merchants, and surge multipliers in real time based on demand forecasts, supply availability, time-of-day patterns, weather, local events, and historical acceptance rates. The system runs pricing decisions every 90 seconds across all active geofenced zones. The problem being solved was manual pricing rules that required 3 FTEs to maintain and update weekly, and which could not respond to real-time conditions — during peak periods, we were seeing courier acceptance rate drops to 38% because rates did not surge fast enough, and during off-peak periods, we were overpricing and losing merchant orders to competitors. After 18 months in production: courier acceptance rate during peak periods averages 78%, off-peak merchant conversion is up 23%, and gross margin per delivery has improved by $0.34 on a base of $2.10. We measure the system's pricing decisions against a simulation of optimal pricing weekly, and track courier satisfaction through a quarterly survey where pricing fairness has improved from 3.1/5 to 4.2/5.

The pricing problem involves three simultaneous optimization objectives that are partially in conflict: courier earnings stability (couriers prefer predictable pay), merchant price sensitivity (lower prices increase merchant order volume), and platform margin. We evaluated five design approaches before committing to the current architecture. A multi-objective optimization with fixed weights was our first approach — it was interpretable and policy-auditable, but the fixed weights caused it to misperform during edge conditions like severe weather where the demand-supply imbalance required rates that violated the fixed weighting. Separate per-objective models were evaluated next — we ran parallel simulations for 3 months; the models achieved higher performance per objective but without a coordination layer they produced conflicting decisions during transition periods. Reinforcement learning was considered and rejected explicitly: the exploration phase would have required operating with non-optimal pricing during learning, which was not acceptable given real income impacts on couriers and real revenue impacts on merchants. A fully human-in-the-loop system was evaluated as a benchmark — 3 FTEs could make better decisions than the automated system during novel conditions but could not operate at the 90-second decision cadence required. We chose a constrained optimization approach with a learned objective function that is updated weekly from outcomes data. The explicit tradeoffs: the system requires weekly human review of the objective function weights, which creates a human bottleneck that limits update frequency; and the system does not learn from out-of-distribution events in real time — its weekly learning cycle means it will underperform for the first 24-48 hours of a genuinely novel market condition.

Our risk register covers 14 items. The five highest-severity: First, the system enters a pricing spiral — surge pricing causes couriers to cluster in high-surge zones, reducing supply elsewhere, which increases surge elsewhere, creating a runaway feedback loop. Mitigation: surge caps are enforced by a separate rule layer that is not part of the optimization; the cap values are set by policy, not by the model, and can be updated in under 5 minutes without a model deployment. Circuit breaker: if aggregate surge above 2x covers more than 25% of active zones for 30 consecutive minutes, the surge cap is automatically reduced by 20% until the condition clears. Second, the system produces pricing decisions that violate local regulations — several of our markets have regulations on surge pricing during declared emergencies. Mitigation: declared emergency status is injected from a city-level API and overrides model pricing with a regulatory cap immediately, without model involvement. This path is tested monthly with a simulated emergency declaration. Third, courier income collapses below minimum wage equivalent due to a model error or data pipeline failure. Mitigation: a floor pricing rule enforces minimum effective hourly rate; if the calculated floor would be triggered more than 5% of active sessions, an alert is sent to the operations team for manual review. Fourth, the system systematically disadvantages certain geographic zones due to training data imbalance — zones with historically lower courier density may receive structurally lower surge rates, reducing courier supply in those zones further in a self-reinforcing pattern. Mitigation: we run a fairness analysis by zone monthly, comparing outcomes for matched demand levels; zones with materially different outcomes trigger a pricing review. Fifth, merchant price sensitivity model fails for a new merchant category with different order characteristics. Mitigation: new merchant categories receive conservative pricing for the first 90 days while the system collects sufficient data; categories are explicitly flagged as 'data-limited' in the pricing dashboard.

Building a system that directly affects income for 12,000 independent workers has made me more systematic and more humble about AI decision-making than any other project I have worked on. The framework I use for AI override decisions in this context is based on a distinction between reversible and irreversible decisions. Reversible decisions — setting a surge multiplier for the next 90 seconds — can be delegated to the system with light oversight because they can be corrected in the next cycle. Irreversible or slow-to-reverse decisions — changing the objective function weights, adjusting floor pricing, modifying zone definitions — require human review regardless of the system's recommendation. This distinction is built into the system's permission structure: the model cannot update objective function weights autonomously; it can only propose changes that are reviewed by a human before deployment. Specific incidents that shaped this: in month 4, the system began underpricing in the eastern zones of one market. The root cause was a data pipeline issue that reduced the apparent demand signal, causing the system to conclude demand was lower than it was. By the time we caught it through a courier acceptance rate alert, 340 couriers had experienced an earnings shortfall of approximately $18 per courier over a 6-hour window. We made those couriers whole through a one-time adjustment and rebuilt our pipeline monitoring to catch data quality issues before they influence pricing. The second major incident was in month 9: the system confidently proposed objective function weight changes during a weekly review that would have favored platform margin at the expense of courier earnings stability. The proposal was framed in terms that sounded like a market efficiency improvement. A team member who was also a former gig worker flagged that the framing obscured what the change would mean for courier income volatility. We rejected the proposal, added a required plain-language translation step to all objective function proposals, and added a courier representative to our pricing review committee. I would not have designed either of these safeguards without the specific incidents that revealed why they were necessary.

rationale: Strong L5 with a few 4s. Boundary earns 4 — market scope and excluded problem types are mentioned but not comprehensively defined. Blast radius earns 4 — impact scope is quantified for each failure mode with containment strategies, but progressive rollback procedures are less explicit than a 5 requires. Override evidence earns 4 — the reversible/irreversible framework is documented and specific incidents shaped it, but the framework was built reactively.
